meta.title: ENT Practice Software | Audiology Coordination & In-Office Procedure Management | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to ENT and otolaryngology practice operations. Audiology testing coordination. In-office procedure optimization. Allergy injection program management. Surgical case coordination.

hero.subhead: Audiology testing needs to be done before the ENT visit, not after. In-office procedures need room visibility and equipment confirmation. Allergy injection programs need systematic scheduling, dose tracking, and vial management. Surgical cases need pre-op coordination. clinIQ manages ENT's multi-service-line complexity.

sections.content:
ENT practices run more distinct service lines simultaneously than almost any specialty. A single practice may encompass: otology (hearing loss, ear surgery), rhinology (sinuses, nasal obstruction, allergy), laryngology (voice, swallowing), head and neck surgery (thyroid, salivary gland, skin cancer), and pediatric ENT (tubes, tonsils, airway). Add in-house audiology and an allergy injection program, and the operational complexity rivals a small hospital service line.

The coordination failures that cost ENT practices money are specific:

• Patients arrive for ENT visits without audiograms — the provider can't make hearing aid or surgical decisions without the data. The visit is clinically incomplete and has to be repeated
• In-office procedures (laryngoscopy, sinus endoscopy, tube placement) require room setup, scope availability, and specific equipment — last-minute scrambling wastes provider time and delays schedules
• Allergy injection programs running hundreds of patients require systematic dose tracking, vial management, and reaction monitoring — without systems, doses get wrong and vials expire
• Surgical scheduling for sinus surgery, myringotomy, tonsillectomy, and thyroid cases requires coordination with facilities, authorization, and pre-operative preparation

A well-run ENT practice can see 40-60 patients per day per provider across these service lines. Operational coordination is the constraint.

Audiology is the diagnostic backbone of ENT practice. Sensorineural hearing loss evaluation, conductive hearing loss workup, unilateral hearing loss assessment, tinnitus evaluation, and pediatric hearing screening all require audiometric testing before the otolaryngologist can make clinical decisions. When testing isn't done before the visit, the encounter is clinically incomplete.

**The audiology coordination failure:**

Patient schedules ENT appointment for hearing loss evaluation. Patient is not told to schedule audiology before the visit, or audiology and ENT are booked separately without communication. Patient arrives for ENT visit without audiogram. Provider examines patient, can't assess degree or type of hearing loss without audiometric data. Visit results in 'please schedule audiology and return' — patient needs two visits instead of one, provider's time is partially wasted, and patient satisfaction suffers.

At 60 patients per day, even 15% who need audiology coordination that isn't done represents 9 patients with incomplete or inefficient visits.

**Audiology testing types and coordination requirements:**

Pure tone audiometry: Standard hearing evaluation — air and bone conduction thresholds across frequencies. Required before any hearing loss management, hearing aid candidacy, or surgical decision.

Tympanometry: Middle ear pressure assessment. Required before ear tube surgery decisions and recurrent otitis media evaluation.

Auditory brainstem response (ABR): For patients with asymmetric hearing loss — requires MRI coordination if acoustic neuroma is suspected.

Otoacoustic emissions: Cochlear hair cell function, neonatal screening.

Vestibular testing (VNG, VEMP, rotary chair): Dizzy patient evaluation — requires separate appointment scheduling with preparation instructions (no caffeine, no sedatives).

**What clinIQ coordinates:**

Pre-visit testing protocol: Visit type determines required audiology testing. ENT appointment for hearing loss triggers automatic audiology order and scheduling prompt. Patient not seen until audiology complete.

Test tracking pipeline: Audiogram ordered → scheduled → completed → result available → ENT visit can proceed.

Hearing aid coordination: For patients proceeding to hearing aids — fitting appointment, dispensing, 30-day trial, follow-up, adjustment — all tracked.

Vestibular testing: Complex vestibular evaluations tracked through complete test battery before physician interpretation visit.

ENT's in-office procedure volume is among the highest of any specialty. Flexible laryngoscopy, nasal endoscopy, cerumen removal, tube placement under microscopy, fine needle aspiration of neck masses, allergy testing, in-office sinus procedures — each requires specific room setup, equipment availability, and time allocation. Managing this procedure volume efficiently determines how many patients can be seen.

**Common ENT in-office procedures and their requirements:**

Flexible laryngoscopy: Flexible scope (clean, lubricated), topical anesthetic spray available, patient seated upright, documentation platform ready. Time: 10-15 minutes including documentation.

Nasal endoscopy: Nasal speculum, endoscope or rigid scope, decongestant and anesthetic spray (typically 4% cocaine or afrin/lidocaine), light source. Time: 5-10 minutes.

Cerumen removal: Ear curette, cerumen spoon, ear irrigation syringe, head lamp or otoscope with light, suction for soft cerumen. Time: 5-15 minutes depending on impaction severity.

Fine needle aspiration: 22-25 gauge needle, multiple syringes, slide preparation materials, pathology transport. Ultrasound guidance equipment if ultrasound-guided. Cytopathologist at facility or send-out coordination.

In-office balloon sinuplasty: Requires specific balloon system kit, endoscope, topical anesthesia protocol, patient education, and post-procedure instructions.

Allergy skin testing: Standardized antigen panel, skin prick lancets or intradermal syringes, measurement materials, epinephrine available, 20-minute observation period.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Procedure room status: Each procedure room shows — setup, occupied, turnover, available. Provider doesn't walk to a room and discover it's occupied or unprepared.

Equipment availability: Scopes are a limited resource. Which scopes are in use, being reprocessed, or available? Procedure scheduling considers scope availability.

Procedure-specific scheduling: Duration-appropriate time blocks. Cerumen removal doesn't need the same slot as balloon sinuplasty. Procedure type drives scheduling template.

Pre-procedure requirements: Consent, patient preparation, equipment confirmation — tracked before the appointment is finalized.

ENT practices with allergy programs run a subspecialty within a subspecialty. Allergy testing, immunotherapy compounding, injection scheduling, dose escalation protocols, reaction monitoring, and vial management are operationally demanding — and clinically essential to get right. A missed dose escalation requires protocol restart. An expired vial wastes compounding cost and delays patient care.

**Allergy program operational components:**

Allergy testing: Skin prick testing or intradermal testing — scheduling, antigen panel selection, results documentation, and immunotherapy indication decision.

Immunotherapy compounding: Custom vials mixed to patient-specific allergen sensitivity profile. Mixing dates, concentrations, escalation volumes, and expiration dates must be tracked for each patient.

Injection scheduling during build-up: Weekly injections for 6-12 months during build-up phase. Each injection at a specific dose and concentration — advancing per protocol if tolerated, stepping back if reaction occurs.

Maintenance injections: Once build-up complete, monthly maintenance injections for 3-5 years. Some patients transition to every 4-8 week intervals.

Reaction documentation: Every injection visit requires 20-30 minute observation. Reactions — local or systemic — documented with severity, intervention, and protocol implications.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Vial inventory: Every patient's vials tracked — mixing date, expiration date, current escalation concentration. Expiring vials generate remix alerts 2 weeks before expiration.

Injection schedule: Each patient's current dose, escalation protocol position, last injection date, next scheduled injection. Missed injections flagged — missed doses have protocol implications (may require going back to previous concentration).

Dose tracking: Current dose and concentration in the escalation protocol. Next dose calculated per protocol. Provider or allergy nurse reviews and approves next dose.

Reaction log: Post-injection reactions documented per patient. Reaction history visible when scheduling next dose — affects dose decision.

Oral immunotherapy (OIT): For practices offering OIT for food allergies — similar dose tracking and escalation management.

ENT surgery spans from quick outpatient procedures (myringotomy with tubes, 10-15 minutes in pediatric patients) to complex oncologic resections requiring multi-hour OR time, reconstruction, and inpatient admission. Each has different coordination requirements.

**High-volume ENT surgical cases:**

Myringotomy with tubes: Often pediatric. Requires parental consent, anesthesia pre-assessment, facility scheduling (often free-standing ASC). High case volume means efficient pre-op coordination at scale.

Tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy: Pediatric and adult. Medical clearance for adult patients with OSA. Facility scheduling, post-op care instructions (pain management, activity restrictions, return precautions).

Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS): Sinus CT correlation with surgical plan, medical clearance, saline irrigation supplies, post-op nasal care protocol.

Septoplasty and rhinoplasty: Conservative management documentation (nasal steroid trial, septum-specific) for insurance authorization. Functional vs. cosmetic documentation.

Thyroid and parathyroid surgery: Labs (TSH, calcium, PTH), laryngoscopy pre-op for vocal cord status, calcium monitoring post-op protocol.

Head and neck oncology: Multi-disciplinary coordination — medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, speech therapy, reconstructive surgery.

**What clinIQ coordinates:**

Surgical pipeline: Every scheduled case with pre-operative checklist completion status. Labs ordered, clearance obtained, imaging reviewed, consent signed, facility confirmed.

Authorization tracking: Septoplasty, sinus surgery, tonsillectomy — authorization requirements by payer. Conservative treatment documentation for septoplasty authorization.

Post-operative schedule: Post-op visits scheduled before patient leaves surgery — 1-week, 2-week, and specialty-specific follow-ups for complex cases.

ENT prior authorization spans multiple procedure categories — in-office procedures requiring auth, surgical cases, and allergy service components.

**Septoplasty authorization:**

Insurance coverage requires documented functional nasal obstruction — not purely cosmetic. Documentation needs: CT sinus showing septal deviation, trial of medical management (nasal steroids, decongestants, nasal strips), objective nasal obstruction assessment. Many payers require Nasal Obstruction Symptom Evaluation (NOSE) scale documentation.

**Sinus surgery authorization:**

Functional endoscopic sinus surgery requires failed medical management — minimum 4-8 weeks of nasal steroids, saline irrigation, antibiotic treatment if bacterial sinusitis — and CT sinus correlation showing the anatomic abnormality. Recurrent acute sinusitis (4+ episodes per year documented) is a separate indication with different documentation requirements.

**Allergy immunotherapy authorization:**

Allergy testing is generally covered with standard allergy coding. Immunotherapy (allergy shots) requires ongoing authorization at many commercial payers — documentation of allergy testing results, physician-supervised program, and ongoing treatment response.

**Hearing aids:**

Hearing aids are generally not covered by commercial payers or Medicare but may be covered by select Advantage plans. Cochlear implant authorization is medical and requires extensive audiometric and medical documentation.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Auth status per procedure per patient: Submitted, pending, approved, denied, appealed. Conservative treatment documentation: Steroid trial, antibiotic courses, saline irrigation — referenced automatically in LOMN. LOMN generation: Templates for septoplasty, FESS, tonsillectomy with auto-populated conservative care history.

**Week 1-2: Configuration**

Audiology coordination workflow built for your visit types. In-office procedure scheduling configured for your procedure mix and room setup. Allergy program loaded with injection protocols and vial management. Surgical authorization tracking configured for your payer mix.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Front desk training on audiology pre-scheduling and procedure booking. Allergy staff training on dose tracking and vial management. Surgical coordinator training on case pipeline and authorization. Provider training on dashboards and workflow.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Audiology coordination immediate — patients arrive with testing done. Procedure room visibility active. Allergy program systematic.

Month 3: Procedure throughput improvement measurable. Allergy injection compliance improved. Surgical authorization approval rate tracking.

Month 6: 25% procedure efficiency improvement documented. Zero expired allergy vials. Zero missed allergy injection doses.

**Annual ROI:**

• Procedure throughput (1-2 additional procedures per day): $100,000-200,000/year at ENT procedure RVU rates
• Allergy program efficiency: Improved retention and compliance
• Surgical authorization approval improvement: $20,000-40,000/year in recovered denials

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including audiology coordination, procedure management, allergy program, surgical authorization
• Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How does audiology coordination work for ENT visits?
faqs.a: Visit types that require audiometric testing trigger an audiology order and scheduling prompt at the time the ENT appointment is booked. Patient is directed to schedule audiology before the ENT visit. Audiology testing completion is tracked — ENT visit is flagged if required testing isn't complete. When the patient arrives, audiology results are available in the chart before the provider sees them.

faqs.q: How is the allergy injection program managed?
faqs.a: Every allergy patient's vials are tracked with mixing date, expiration date, current dose, and escalation position. Injection schedules are managed per patient — weekly during build-up, monthly during maintenance. Missed injections are flagged with protocol implications (missed doses may require dose reduction). Vials approaching expiration trigger remix alerts 2 weeks before the expiration date. Post-injection reactions are documented with protocol impact.

faqs.q: How do you track in-office procedure room availability?
faqs.a: Each procedure room shows real-time status — setup in progress, occupied, turnover, available. Equipment (scopes, specific procedure kits) availability is tracked. Providers see which rooms are ready before walking there. Scheduling confirms room and equipment availability before booking — no discovering an occupied room mid-session.

faqs.q: What LOMN templates do you have for ENT surgery?
faqs.a: clinIQ includes LOMN templates for septoplasty (with medical management documentation, NOSE scale data, CT correlation), FESS (with failed medical management and CT sinuses), tonsillectomy, and thyroid surgery. Conservative treatment history — steroid trials, antibiotic courses, saline irrigation — is auto-populated from visit documentation.

faqs.q: How does hearing aid coordination work?
faqs.a: For patients who are hearing aid candidates, clinIQ tracks the hearing aid pipeline: audiometric evaluation complete, candidacy determination, hearing aid selection, fitting appointment, dispense, 30-day trial period, follow-up, and adjustment appointments. The pipeline ensures no patient is lost between audiometric diagnosis and hearing aid dispensing.

faqs.q: How is the septoplasty authorization documented?
faqs.a: Septoplasty authorization requires nasal obstruction documentation (NOSE scale or similar), CT sinus showing deviation, and conservative management trial — nasal steroids, decongestants, nasal strips, duration of trial. clinIQ tracks medical management at each visit and generates LOMN with auto-populated conservative treatment history. First-submission approval rates improve significantly with complete documentation.

faqs.q: Can allergy and ENT workflows exist in the same system?
faqs.a: Yes. Allergy patients and ENT patients share the same practice platform. Allergy-specific workflows (injection scheduling, vial management, dose escalation) are distinct from ENT surgical coordination and audiology workflows. The system supports the multi-service-line reality of modern ENT practices without requiring separate systems for each service line.

Two weeks. Week 1: audiology coordination workflow, allergy program setup (injection protocols, vial management), procedure room configuration, surgical authorization templates. Week 2: staff training by role — allergy nurses, surgical coordinators, front desk, providers — and parallel testing. Week 3: live with support.

testimonial.quote: Patients used to arrive for ENT visits without their audiograms — that visit was always wasted. Now everything is coordinated before they arrive. And the allergy program finally has visibility.
